plainMeaning: I praise Lord Śānti, the peace-maker who calms every fear — may he grant peace to me.
recitation: taṁ saṁti saṁtikaraṁ, saṁtiṇaṁ savvabhayā | saṁti thuṇāmi jiṇaṁ, saṁti viheu me ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit accusative singular of the demonstrative tad (Skt. tam); points to Śāntinātha just portrayed.
wordByWord.notes: Object of thuṇāmi ('I praise that one').

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit for Skt. śānti, 'peace'; here the proper name of the 16th Jina, by the hymn's title-pun simultaneously meaning 'peace' itself.
wordByWord.notes: First of five occurrences of saṁti in the quatrain.

wordByWord.etymology: saṁti (śānti, 'peace') + karaṁ (Skt. -kara, 'maker, doer', from √kṛ); accusative agreeing with taṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Epithet: the one who actively produces peace.

wordByWord.etymology: From √śam ('to be calm') / śānti; agent-sense 'one who pacifies', construed with savvabhayā.
wordByWord.notes: Ambiguous form: read here as agentive ('pacifier of all fear'); see notes for the alternate genitive-plural reading 'of the peaceful'.

wordByWord.etymology: savva (Skt. sarva, 'all') + bhayā (Skt. bhaya, 'fear'); Prakrit ablative/accusative-plural sense, 'from / [of] every fear'.
wordByWord.notes: Picks up the hymn's abhaya (fearlessness) motif — Śānti calms every dread.

wordByWord.etymology: Repetition of saṁti (śānti); here qualifying jiṇaṁ, 'the Jina who is Peace'.
wordByWord.notes: Third utterance; binds the name to the object of praise.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit 1st person singular present of √thuṇa = Skt. √stu (stunāmi), 'to praise'; s- > th- by Prakrit sound-law.
wordByWord.notes: The hymn's act of stuti voiced in the first person.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit accusative singular of jiṇa (Skt. jina, 'conqueror' of passions), from √ji, 'to conquer'.
wordByWord.notes: Object of thuṇāmi, in apposition to taṁ and saṁti.

wordByWord.etymology: saṁti (Skt. śānti, 'peace, tranquillity'); here the abstract boon, object of viheu.
wordByWord.notes: Fifth occurrence; the name has resolved fully into the thing prayed for.

wordByWord.gloss: may he bestow, let him grant
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit 3rd person singular imperative of vi-√dhā (Skt. vidadhātu), 'let him make / grant / ordain'.
wordByWord.notes: Turns praise into petition; the verse's only imperative.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit enclitic dative/genitive of the 1st person (Skt. me), 'to/of me'.
wordByWord.notes: Names the beneficiary of the prayed-for peace.

literalTranslation: Him — Śānti, the peace-maker, the pacifier of every fear — that Jina Śānti I praise; may he grant peace to me.
idiomaticTranslation: That Lord Śānti — maker of peace, stiller of every fear — that Jina, who is Peace itself, I now extol: may he bestow his peace on me.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This quatrain is the hymn's structural payoff: having raised Śānti's royal and cosmic majesty, the poet now turns the title's bivalent pun into direct petition. The syllable saṁti tolls five times, and each toll shifts register — first the Tīrthaṅkara's name (taṁ saṁti, 'that Śānti'), then his epithet of agency (saṁtikaraṁ, 'peace-maker'), then his power over dread (saṁtiṇaṁ savvabhayā, 'who stills every fear'), then the named object of worship (saṁti... jiṇaṁ, 'the Jina who is Peace'), and finally the boon itself (saṁti viheu me, 'may he grant me peace'). The repetition is not redundancy but devotional concentration: speech is saturated with one sound until name, quality, and prayer collapse into a single utterance, and to say 'Śānti' is already to ask for śānti. Notice the grammatical hinge. The first three lines are all accusatives of praise (taṁ, saṁtikaraṁ, jiṇaṁ — the one extolled by thuṇāmi); the last clause pivots to the lone imperative viheu, so adoration modulates seamlessly into request. savvabhayā gathers up the abhaya thread the hymn has been weaving — the Jina who quells all fear is now asked to quell this reciter's own — and me makes the cosmic petition personal: the universal pacifier is begged for one trembling self.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The imperative viheu me ('may he grant me peace') must be read through the vītarāga principle: a Jina, free of attachment and aversion, neither intervenes nor dispenses favours. The 'granting' is therefore aspirational, not transactional — peace arises in the devotee from the purifying contemplation of one who has himself conquered (jiṇa) the inner enemies, so that praising Śānti is the very mechanism by which śānti is realised. The fear (bhaya) stilled here is not merely circumstantial dread but the existential insecurity of saṁsāra; abhaya, fearlessness, is the natural fruit of passions burnt out. Thus the verse compresses the hymn's whole soteriology into one prayer: the Victor's quenched fear becomes the model and the means of the reciter's own.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited within the protective smaraṇa portion, this is the verse to dwell on when fear or agitation must be settled before reflection. Let the fivefold saṁti work as a calming refrain — slow the breath on each repetition — so that, before confessing the period's faults, the mind is steadied by the one it praises and the petition viheu me becomes a sincere inward resolve for equanimity.

crossReferences.text: Logassa: 'ārugga-bohi-lābhaṁ samāhi-varam-uttamaṁ diṁtu' — the parallel petition asking the vītarāga Jinas to 'grant' health, enlightenment, and supreme samādhi.
Refrain 'namo ajiya saṁtiṇaṁ' (v.3) — the same name-play of which this verse is the petitionary culmination.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary (Ajitaśānti Stava recensions and stotra anthologies)
sources.consultedFor: Verse identification, Śāntinātha as object of vv.9–12, and the name-play culminating in a direct prayer for peace.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Roots underlying thuṇāmi (√stu, stunāmi 'I praise') and viheu (vi-√dhā, vidadhātu 'let him grant/make'); śānti, sarva, bhaya, jina.

sources.title: H. T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit forms thuṇai/thuṇāmi, jiṇa, saṁti, savva, bhaya, and the imperative formation viheu.

notes: saṁtiṇaṁ is grammatically ambiguous: rendered here as an agentive ('pacifier of/from all fear') governing savvabhayā; some recensions parse it as a genitive plural 'of the peaceful ones', and savvabhayā as ablative 'from all fear' rather than accusative plural 'all fears' — both yield the same devotional sense, so the variance is noted, not resolved. The imperative viheu me ('may he grant me peace') is read in the vītarāga sense (aspiration, not theistic boon-giving). Orthography (single/doubled conjuncts, anusvāra, the bare daṇḍa) is preserved verbatim from the book and not normalised. The widely circulated continuation 'saṁti maṁgala-mayaṁ, saṁti kallāṇa-ṇāṇa' belongs to a longer/variant recension and is not part of this book's verse 12; it is excluded.
